Plan on making most of the projects.", "review_time": "2020-02-27 05:11:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_76"}, {"rating": "2", "title": "Heroine blames others for things & feels her bad behavior is justified", "text": "\"Dead Silence\" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

I received an ebook review copy of this book from the publisher through NetGalley.", "review_time": "2020-06-01 07:33:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_167"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Book of Love series", "text": "Enjoyed reading another story about a Farthingale cousin who finds true love. Looking forward to the next book.", "review_time": "2021-07-31 18:34:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_23"}, {"rating": "2", "title": "Overpriced for the size", "text": "Book is small and feels cheap. I thought it was going to be a bigger book for kiddos.", "review_time": "2021-01-27 07:08:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_99"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Murder, Kidnapping, and Prayers", "text": "There are so many things to love about \"Dead Silence,\" by Robin Caroll. First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about \"Dead Silence\" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.", "review_time": "2020-06-14 11:52:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_167"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "A Most Entertaining and enjoyable book.", "text": "There were so many twists and turns to this book. function.arguments:
{"code": "import json

# Load children books data (this is not a file path, but direct result)
children_books = var_functions.query_db:20

# Load reviews data from file
file_path = var_functions.query_db:22
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Debug info
print(f'Children books: {len(children_books)}')
print(f'Reviews from 2020+: {len(reviews_data)}')

# Map book IDs to titles
book_titles = {book['book_id']: book['title'] for book in children_books}

# Calculate average ratings for children's books
book_ratings = {}

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Check if this is a children's book
    if purchase_id in book_titles:
        if purchase_id not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[purchase_id] = []
        book_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)

# Calculate averages and filter 
qualified = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if ratings:
        avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg >= 4.5 and len(ratings) >= 2:  # At least 2 reviews
            qualified.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_id],
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(avg, 2),
                'review_count': len(ratings)
            })

qualified.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], x['review_count']))

# Show results
print('\nQualified Children\'s Books (avg rating >= 4.5, from 2020+):')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"{book['title']} ({book['review_count']} reviews) - {book['average_rating']}")

# Prepare result for return 
__result__ = []
if qualified:
    __result__ = qualified
else:
    __result__ = 'No books found meeting criteria'

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(__result__))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json

# Access stored results via locals()
children_books_key = 'var_functions.query_db:20'
reviews_file_key = 'var_functions.query_db:22'

children_books = locals()[children_books_key]
reviews_file_path = locals()[reviews_file_key]

# Load reviews data
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Debug info
print(f'Children books: {len(children_books)}')
print(f'Reviews from 2020+: {len(reviews_data)}')

# Map book IDs to titles
book_titles = {}
for book in children_books:
    book_titles[book['book_id']] = book['title']

# Calculate ratings for children's books
book_ratings = {}

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    if purchase_id in book_titles:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        if purchase_id not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[purchase_id] = []
        book_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)

# Filter books with avg rating >= 4.5 and at least 2 reviews
qualified = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if len(ratings) >= 2:
        avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg >= 4.5:
            qualified.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_id],
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(avg, 2),
                'review_count': len(ratings)
            })

qualified.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], -x['review_count']))

print('\nQualified Children Books (avg rating >= 4.5, from 2020+):')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"  {book['title']} ({book['review_count']} reviews) - {book['average_rating']}")

result = qualified if qualified else 'No books found meeting criteria'
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json

# Get children's books
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
reviews_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']

with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Create lookup for children's book IDs
children_book_ids = [book['book_id'] for book in children_books]
book_titles = {book['book_id']: book['title'] for book in children_books}

# Check which children's books have reviews
children_reviews = []
for review in reviews_2020:
    if review['purchase_id'] in children_book_ids:
        children_reviews.append(review)

# Group by book
from collections import defaultdict
book_rating_groups = defaultdict(list)
for review in children_reviews:
    book_id = review['purchase_id']
    book_rating_groups[book_id].append(float(review['rating']))

# Calculate stats for each book
book_stats = []
for book_id, ratings in book_rating_groups.items():
    avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
    book_stats.append({
        'title': book_titles[book_id],
        'book_id': book_id,
        'avg_rating': round(avg, 2),
        'review_count': len(ratings)
    })

# Sort by review count (most reviewed first)
book_stats.sort(key=lambda x: -x['review_count'])

print(f'Found {len(book_stats)} children books with reviews from 2020+:')
for book in book_stats:
    print(f"  {book['title']}")
    print(f"    Avg: {book['avg_rating']}, Reviews: {book['review_count']}")

# Now filter for >= 4.5
qualified = [b for b in book_stats if b['avg_rating'] >= 4.5]
print(f'\nBooks with avg >= 4.5: {len(qualified)}')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"  {book['title']} - {book['avg_rating']} ({book['review_count']} reviews)")

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(qualified))"}

# Get the stored results
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
reviews_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']

# Load reviews data
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Create lookup for children's books
children_book_ids = set()
book_titles = {}
for book in children_books:
    children_book_ids.add(book['book_id'])
    book_titles[book['book_id']] = book['title']

# Filter reviews for children's books only
children_reviews = []
for review in reviews_2020:
    if review['purchase_id'] in children_book_ids:
        children_reviews.append(review)

# Group ratings by book
book_ratings = {}
for review in children_reviews:
    book_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    if book_id not in book_ratings:
        book_ratings[book_id] = []
    book_ratings[book_id].append(rating)

# Calculate average ratings and filter
qualified_books = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    review_count = len(ratings)
    if review_count >= 2:  # At least 2 reviews
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / review_count
        if avg_rating >= 4.5:
            qualified_books.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_id],
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
                'review_count': review_count
            })

# Sort by average rating descending, then by review count
qualified_books.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], -x['review_count']))

result_data = qualified_books if qualified_books else 'No books found meeting the criteria'

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result_data))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json

# Load reviews data
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    all_reviews = json.load(f)

# Get children's books
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']

# Map book IDs to titles
id_to_title = {}
for book in children_books:
    id_to_title[book['book_id']] = book['title']

# Group reviews by purchase_id
reviews_by_book = {}
for review in all_reviews:
    pid = review['purchase_id']
    if pid not in reviews_by_book:
        reviews_by_book[pid] = []
    reviews_by_book[pid].append(float(review['rating']))

# Check which children's books have reviews and calculate their averages
children_with_reviews = []
for book_id in id_to_title.keys():
    if book_id in reviews_by_book:
        ratings = reviews_by_book[book_id]
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        children_with_reviews.append({
            'title': id_to_title[book_id],
            'book_id': book_id,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'review_count': len(ratings)
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
children_with_reviews.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print(f'Found {len(children_with_reviews)} children books with reviews from 2020+:')
for book in children_with_reviews:
    print(f"  {book['title']} - Avg: {book['avg_rating']}, Reviews: {book['review_count']}")

# Filter for >= 4.5
qualified_books = [b for b in children_with_reviews if b['avg_rating'] >= 4.5]
print(f'\nBooks with avg rating >= 4.5: {len(qualified_books)}')

result = qualified_books if qualified_books else []
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))"}

Let me check the actual data more carefully. Maybe the issue is that:
1. The ratings are strings that need to be converted to floats
2. The purchase_id format might not match the book_id format exactly
3. The review threshold might be too strict

Since the result came back empty, I should verify:
- Are there any children's books that have reviews at all?
- What are the actual average ratings?
- Maybe some books have exactly 4.5 or higher but with only 1 review?

Let me recalculate with more debugging to understand what's actually in the data.

You are a data analysis agent. Use only the tools listed below to answer the user's query, based on the provided DATABASE DESCRIPTION for logical database names and their types (SQL or MongoDB), and the results of previous tool calls.

TOOLS (system will execute):
- query_db: run a SQL or Mongo query. Returns a list of JSON-serializable records or an error string.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>", "query": "<SQL or Mongo query>"}
- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.
3. For query_db, always specify db_name and query. Refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION for db_name and query format.
4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

EXAMPLES:
- query_db:
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "{"collection": "some_collection", "filter": {some_filter}, "projection": {some_projection}, "limit": some_limit}"}} # for MongoDB databases

- list_db:
{"tool": "list_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name"}}

- execute_python:
{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage
df1 = pd.DataFrame(rl1)
df2 = pd.DataFrame(rl2)
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id').head(10).to_json(orient='records')
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result)"}}

- return_answer:
{"tool": "return_answer", "args": {"answer": "…final plain-text answer…"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which books categorized as 'Children's Books' have received an average rating of at least 4.5 based on reviews from 2020 onwards?

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

2. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Review Title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_info table in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_info.

Plan on making most of the projects.", "review_time": "2020-02-27 05:11:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_76"}, {"rating": "2", "title": "Heroine blames others for things & feels her bad behavior is justified", "text": "\"Dead Silence\" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

I received an ebook review copy of this book from the publisher through NetGalley.", "review_time": "2020-06-01 07:33:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_167"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Book of Love series", "text": "Enjoyed reading another story about a Farthingale cousin who finds true love. Looking forward to the next book.", "review_time": "2021-07-31 18:34:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_23"}, {"rating": "2", "title": "Overpriced for the size", "text": "Book is small and feels cheap. I thought it was going to be a bigger book for kiddos.", "review_time": "2021-01-27 07:08:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_99"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Murder, Kidnapping, and Prayers", "text": "There are so many things to love about \"Dead Silence,\" by Robin Caroll. First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about \"Dead Silence\" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.", "review_time": "2020-06-14 11:52:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_167"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "A Most Entertaining and enjoyable book.", "text": "There were so many twists and turns to this book. function.arguments:
{"code": "import json

# Load children books data (this is not a file path, but direct result)
children_books = var_functions.query_db:20

# Load reviews data from file
file_path = var_functions.query_db:22
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Debug info
print(f'Children books: {len(children_books)}')
print(f'Reviews from 2020+: {len(reviews_data)}')

# Map book IDs to titles
book_titles = {book['book_id']: book['title'] for book in children_books}

# Calculate average ratings for children's books
book_ratings = {}

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Check if this is a children's book
    if purchase_id in book_titles:
        if purchase_id not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[purchase_id] = []
        book_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)

# Calculate averages and filter 
qualified = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if ratings:
        avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg >= 4.5 and len(ratings) >= 2:  # At least 2 reviews
            qualified.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_id],
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(avg, 2),
                'review_count': len(ratings)
            })

qualified.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], x['review_count']))

# Show results
print('\nQualified Children\'s Books (avg rating >= 4.5, from 2020+):')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"{book['title']} ({book['review_count']} reviews) - {book['average_rating']}")

# Prepare result for return 
__result__ = []
if qualified:
    __result__ = qualified
else:
    __result__ = 'No books found meeting criteria'

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(__result__))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json

# Access stored results via locals()
children_books_key = 'var_functions.query_db:20'
reviews_file_key = 'var_functions.query_db:22'

children_books = locals()[children_books_key]
reviews_file_path = locals()[reviews_file_key]

# Load reviews data
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Debug info
print(f'Children books: {len(children_books)}')
print(f'Reviews from 2020+: {len(reviews_data)}')

# Map book IDs to titles
book_titles = {}
for book in children_books:
    book_titles[book['book_id']] = book['title']

# Calculate ratings for children's books
book_ratings = {}

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    if purchase_id in book_titles:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        if purchase_id not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[purchase_id] = []
        book_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)

# Filter books with avg rating >= 4.5 and at least 2 reviews
qualified = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if len(ratings) >= 2:
        avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg >= 4.5:
            qualified.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_id],
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(avg, 2),
                'review_count': len(ratings)
            })

qualified.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], -x['review_count']))

print('\nQualified Children Books (avg rating >= 4.5, from 2020+):')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"  {book['title']} ({book['review_count']} reviews) - {book['average_rating']}")

result = qualified if qualified else 'No books found meeting criteria'
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json

# Get children's books
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
reviews_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']

with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Create lookup for children's book IDs
children_book_ids = [book['book_id'] for book in children_books]
book_titles = {book['book_id']: book['title'] for book in children_books}

# Check which children's books have reviews
children_reviews = []
for review in reviews_2020:
    if review['purchase_id'] in children_book_ids:
        children_reviews.append(review)

# Group by book
from collections import defaultdict
book_rating_groups = defaultdict(list)
for review in children_reviews:
    book_id = review['purchase_id']
    book_rating_groups[book_id].append(float(review['rating']))

# Calculate stats for each book
book_stats = []
for book_id, ratings in book_rating_groups.items():
    avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
    book_stats.append({
        'title': book_titles[book_id],
        'book_id': book_id,
        'avg_rating': round(avg, 2),
        'review_count': len(ratings)
    })

# Sort by review count (most reviewed first)
book_stats.sort(key=lambda x: -x['review_count'])

print(f'Found {len(book_stats)} children books with reviews from 2020+:')
for book in book_stats:
    print(f"  {book['title']}")
    print(f"    Avg: {book['avg_rating']}, Reviews: {book['review_count']}")

# Now filter for >= 4.5
qualified = [b for b in book_stats if b['avg_rating'] >= 4.5]
print(f'\nBooks with avg >= 4.5: {len(qualified)}')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"  {book['title']} - {book['avg_rating']} ({book['review_count']} reviews)")

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(qualified))"}

{"code": "import json

# Get the stored results
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
reviews_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']

# Load reviews data
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Create lookup for children's books
children_book_ids = set()
book_titles = {}
for book in children_books:
    children_book_ids.add(book['book_id'])
    book_titles[book['book_id']] = book['title']

# Filter reviews for children's books only
children_reviews = []
for review in reviews_2020:
    if review['purchase_id'] in children_book_ids:
        children_reviews.append(review)

# Group ratings by book
book_ratings = {}
for review in children_reviews:
    book_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    if book_id not in book_ratings:
        book_ratings[book_id] = []
    book_ratings[book_id].append(rating)

# Calculate average ratings and filter
qualified_books = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    review_count = len(ratings)
    if review_count >= 2:  # At least 2 reviews
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / review_count
        if avg_rating >= 4.5:
            qualified_books.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_id],
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
                'review_count': review_count
            })

# Sort by average rating descending, then by review count
qualified_books.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], -x['review_count']))

result_data = qualified_books if qualified_books else 'No books found meeting the criteria'

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result_data))"}

Which books categorized as 'Children's Books' have received an average rating of at least 4.5 based on reviews from 2020 onwards?

   - This database consists of one table:
    - review
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Review Title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_info table in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_info.

It's the best I've seen.", "review_time": "2020-08-12 11:06:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_8"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Greet book", "text": "Lots of great information. Plan on making most of the projects.", "review_time": "2020-02-27 05:11:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_76"}, {"rating": "2", "title": "Heroine blames others for things & feels her bad behavior is justified", "text": "\"Dead Silence\" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

I received an ebook review copy of this book from the publisher through NetGalley.", "review_time": "2020-06-01 07:33:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_167"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Book of Love series", "text": "Enjoyed reading another story about a Farthingale cousin who finds true love. Looking forward to the next book.", "review_time": "2021-07-31 18:34:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_23"}, {"rating": "2", "title": "Overpriced for the size", "text": "Book is small and feels cheap. I thought it was going to be a bigger book for kiddos.", "review_time": "2021-01-27 07:08:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_99"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Murder, Kidnapping, and Prayers", "text": "There are so many things to love about \"Dead Silence,\" by Robin Caroll. First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about \"Dead Silence\" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.", "review_time": "2020-06-14 11:52:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_167"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "A Most Entertaining and enjoyable book.", "text": "There were so many twists and turns to this book. A great \u201cwho-done-it\u201d. It certainly held my attention. She loves the stories that go along with the recipes, has already earmarked several recipes to try. Apparently the author has other books about French cooking and she is considering buying those as well. I had hoped for a more detailed, in-depth discussion of the genesis and development of this iconic racing engine, and more on the personalities of the principles. function.arguments:
{"code": "import json

# Load children books data (this is not a file path, but direct result)
children_books = var_functions.query_db:20

# Load reviews data from file
file_path = var_functions.query_db:22
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Debug info
print(f'Children books: {len(children_books)}')
print(f'Reviews from 2020+: {len(reviews_data)}')

# Map book IDs to titles
book_titles = {book['book_id']: book['title'] for book in children_books}

# Calculate average ratings for children's books
book_ratings = {}

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Check if this is a children's book
    if purchase_id in book_titles:
        if purchase_id not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[purchase_id] = []
        book_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)

# Calculate averages and filter 
qualified = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if ratings:
        avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg >= 4.5 and len(ratings) >= 2:  # At least 2 reviews
            qualified.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_id],
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(avg, 2),
                'review_count': len(ratings)
            })

qualified.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], x['review_count']))

# Show results
print('\nQualified Children\'s Books (avg rating >= 4.5, from 2020+):')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"{book['title']} ({book['review_count']} reviews) - {book['average_rating']}")

# Prepare result for return 
__result__ = []
if qualified:
    __result__ = qualified
else:
    __result__ = 'No books found meeting criteria'

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(__result__))"}

# Map book IDs to titles

for book in children_books:
    book_titles[book['book_id']] = book['title']

# Calculate ratings for children's books
book_ratings = {}

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    if purchase_id in book_titles:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        if purchase_id not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[purchase_id] = []
        book_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)

# Filter books with avg rating >= 4.5 and at least 2 reviews
qualified = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if len(ratings) >= 2:
        avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg >= 4.5:
            qualified.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_id],
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(avg, 2),
                'review_count': len(ratings)
            })

qualified.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], -x['review_count']))

print('\nQualified Children Books (avg rating >= 4.5, from 2020+):')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"  {book['title']} ({book['review_count']} reviews) - {book['average_rating']}")

result = qualified if qualified else 'No books found meeting criteria'
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))"}

{"code": "import json

# Get children's books
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
reviews_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']

with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Create lookup for children's book IDs
children_book_ids = [book['book_id'] for book in children_books]
book_titles = {book['book_id']: book['title'] for book in children_books}

# Check which children's books have reviews
children_reviews = []
for review in reviews_2020:
    if review['purchase_id'] in children_book_ids:
        children_reviews.append(review)

# Group by book
from collections import defaultdict
book_rating_groups = defaultdict(list)
for review in children_reviews:
    book_id = review['purchase_id']
    book_rating_groups[book_id].append(float(review['rating']))

# Calculate stats for each book
book_stats = []
for book_id, ratings in book_rating_groups.items():
    avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
    book_stats.append({
        'title': book_titles[book_id],
        'book_id': book_id,
        'avg_rating': round(avg, 2),
        'review_count': len(ratings)
    })

# Sort by review count (most reviewed first)
book_stats.sort(key=lambda x: -x['review_count'])

print(f'Found {len(book_stats)} children books with reviews from 2020+:')
for book in book_stats:
    print(f"  {book['title']}")
    print(f"    Avg: {book['avg_rating']}, Reviews: {book['review_count']}")

# Now filter for >= 4.5
qualified = [b for b in book_stats if b['avg_rating'] >= 4.5]
print(f'\nBooks with avg >= 4.5: {len(qualified)}')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"  {book['title']} - {book['avg_rating']} ({book['review_count']} reviews)")

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(qualified))"}

I'll investigate converting the purchase IDs to match the book ID format. I'll strip the "purchaseid_" prefix and add the "bookid_" prefix to create a direct comparison. This approach might reveal the hidden connection between the two ID sets. By transforming the purchase IDs, I could potentially align them with the book IDs and solve the matching challenge.

My next step is to systematically map the purchase IDs to the book ID format, creating a potential bridge between the two datasets. I'll carefully replace the prefixes and test the matching strategy to see if I can uncover the relationship hinted at in the original problem.

You are a data analysis agent. Use only the tools listed below to answer the user's query, based on the provided DATABASE DESCRIPTION for logical database names and their types (SQL or MongoDB), and the results of previous tool calls.

TOOLS (system will execute):
- query_db: run a SQL or Mongo query. Returns a list of JSON-serializable records or an error string.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>", "query": "<SQL or Mongo query>"}
- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.
3. For query_db, always specify db_name and query. Refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION for db_name and query format.
4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

EXAMPLES:
- query_db:
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "{"collection": "some_collection", "filter": {some_filter}, "projection": {some_projection}, "limit": some_limit}"}} # for MongoDB databases

- list_db:
{"tool": "list_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name"}}

- execute_python:
{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage
df1 = pd.DataFrame(rl1)
df2 = pd.DataFrame(rl2)
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id').head(10).to_json(orient='records')
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result)"}}

- return_answer:
{"tool": "return_answer", "args": {"answer": "…final plain-text answer…"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which books categorized as 'Children's Books' have received an average rating of at least 4.5 based on reviews from 2020 onwards?

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

2. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Review Title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_info table in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_info.

Plan on making most of the projects.", "review_time": "2020-02-27 05:11:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_76"}, {"rating": "2", "title": "Heroine blames others for things & feels her bad behavior is justified", "text": "\"Dead Silence\" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about \"Dead Silence\" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.", "review_time": "2020-06-14 11:52:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_167"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "A Most Entertaining and enjoyable book.", "text": "There were so many twists and turns to this book. # Load reviews data from file

# Debug info

print(f'Reviews from 2020+: {len(reviews_data)}')

# Map book IDs to titles
book_titles = {book['book_id']: book['title'] for book in children_books}

# Calculate average ratings for children's books
book_ratings = {}

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Check if this is a children's book
    if purchase_id in book_titles:
        if purchase_id not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[purchase_id] = []
        book_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)

# Calculate averages and filter

for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():

        avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg >= 4.5 and len(ratings) >= 2:  # At least 2 reviews

                'average_rating': round(avg, 2),

            })

qualified.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], x['review_count']))

# Show results

# Prepare result for return

if qualified:

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json

# Get children's books
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
reviews_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']

with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Create lookup for children's book IDs
children_book_ids = [book['book_id'] for book in children_books]
book_titles = {book['book_id']: book['title'] for book in children_books}

# Check which children's books have reviews
children_reviews = []
for review in reviews_2020:
    if review['purchase_id'] in children_book_ids:
        children_reviews.append(review)

# Group by book
from collections import defaultdict
book_rating_groups = defaultdict(list)
for review in children_reviews:
    book_id = review['purchase_id']
    book_rating_groups[book_id].append(float(review['rating']))

# Calculate stats for each book
book_stats = []
for book_id, ratings in book_rating_groups.items():
    avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
    book_stats.append({
        'title': book_titles[book_id],
        'book_id': book_id,
        'avg_rating': round(avg, 2),
        'review_count': len(ratings)
    })

# Sort by review count (most reviewed first)
book_stats.sort(key=lambda x: -x['review_count'])

print(f'Found {len(book_stats)} children books with reviews from 2020+:')
for book in book_stats:
    print(f"  {book['title']}")
    print(f"    Avg: {book['avg_rating']}, Reviews: {book['review_count']}")

# Now filter for >= 4.5
qualified = [b for b in book_stats if b['avg_rating'] >= 4.5]
print(f'\nBooks with avg >= 4.5: {len(qualified)}')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"  {book['title']} - {book['avg_rating']} ({book['review_count']} reviews)")

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(qualified))"}

# Load reviews data

book_titles = {}
for book in children_books:
    children_book_ids.add(book['book_id'])
    book_titles[book['book_id']] = book['title']

# Filter reviews for children's books only
children_reviews = []
for review in reviews_2020:
    if review['purchase_id'] in children_book_ids:
        children_reviews.append(review)

# Group ratings by book
book_ratings = {}
for review in children_reviews:
    book_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    if book_id not in book_ratings:
        book_ratings[book_id] = []
    book_ratings[book_id].append(rating)

# Calculate average ratings and filter
qualified_books = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    review_count = len(ratings)
    if review_count >= 2:  # At least 2 reviews
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / review_count
        if avg_rating >= 4.5:
            qualified_books.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_id],
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
                'review_count': review_count
            })

# Sort by average rating descending, then by review count
qualified_books.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], -x['review_count']))

result_data = qualified_books if qualified_books else 'No books found meeting the criteria'

# Check if any start with 'bookid'

print(f'Purchase_ids starting with bookid: {len(bookid_purchases)}')
print('bookid purchases:', bookid_purchases[:20])

# Get children's books IDs
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
children_book_ids = [book['book_id'] for book in children_books]
print(f'Children book IDs: {children_book_ids[:10]}')

# Find intersection

print(f'Intersection (books with reviews): {len(intersection)}')

# Check reviews for children books
children_reviews = [r for r in reviews if r['purchase_id'] in children_book_ids]
print(f'Reviews for children books: {len(children_reviews)}')

# Group by book
from collections import defaultdict
book_reviews = defaultdict(list)
for review in children_reviews:
    book_reviews[review['purchase_id']].append(float(review['rating']))

print('\nBook review counts:')
for book_id, ratings in sorted(book_reviews.items(), key=lambda x: len(x[1]), reverse=True):

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json

# Load reviews data
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    all_reviews = json.load(f)

# Get children's books
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']

# Map book IDs to titles
id_to_title = {}
for book in children_books:
    id_to_title[book['book_id']] = book['title']

# Group reviews by purchase_id
reviews_by_book = {}
for review in all_reviews:
    pid = review['purchase_id']
    if pid not in reviews_by_book:
        reviews_by_book[pid] = []
    reviews_by_book[pid].append(float(review['rating']))

# Check which children's books have reviews and calculate their averages
children_with_reviews = []
for book_id in id_to_title.keys():
    if book_id in reviews_by_book:
        ratings = reviews_by_book[book_id]
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        children_with_reviews.append({
            'title': id_to_title[book_id],
            'book_id': book_id,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'review_count': len(ratings)
        })

# Sort by average rating descending
children_with_reviews.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print(f'Found {len(children_with_reviews)} children books with reviews from 2020+:')
for book in children_with_reviews:
    print(f"  {book['title']} - Avg: {book['avg_rating']}, Reviews: {book['review_count']}")

# Filter for >= 4.5
qualified_books = [b for b in children_with_reviews if b['avg_rating'] >= 4.5]
print(f'\nBooks with avg rating >= 4.5: {len(qualified_books)}')

result = qualified_books if qualified_books else []
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))"}

QUERY:
Which books categorized as 'Children's Books' have received an average rating of at least 4.5 based on reviews from 2020 onwards?

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

2. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Review Title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_info table in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_info.

Many projects to make. Easy instructions. Love the patterns. Plan on making most of the projects.", "review_time": "2020-02-27 05:11:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_76"}, {"rating": "2", "title": "Heroine blames others for things & feels her bad behavior is justified", "text": "\"Dead Silence\" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

I received an ebook review copy of this book from the publisher through NetGalley.", "review_time": "2020-06-01 07:33:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_167"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Book of Love series", "text": "Enjoyed reading another story about a Farthingale cousin who finds true love. Looking forward to the next book.", "review_time": "2021-07-31 18:34:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_23"}, {"rating": "2", "title": "Overpriced for the size", "text": "Book is small and feels cheap. I thought it was going to be a bigger book for kiddos.", "review_time": "2021-01-27 07:08:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_99"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Murder, Kidnapping, and Prayers", "text": "There are so many things to love about \"Dead Silence,\" by Robin Caroll. First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about \"Dead Silence\" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.", "review_time": "2020-06-14 11:52:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_167"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "A Most Entertaining and enjoyable book.", "text": "There were so many twists and turns to this book. function.arguments:
{"code": "import json

# Load children books data (this is not a file path, but direct result)
children_books = var_functions.query_db:20

# Load reviews data from file
file_path = var_functions.query_db:22
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Debug info
print(f'Children books: {len(children_books)}')
print(f'Reviews from 2020+: {len(reviews_data)}')

# Map book IDs to titles
book_titles = {book['book_id']: book['title'] for book in children_books}

# Calculate average ratings for children's books
book_ratings = {}

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Check if this is a children's book
    if purchase_id in book_titles:
        if purchase_id not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[purchase_id] = []
        book_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)

# Calculate averages and filter 
qualified = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if ratings:
        avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg >= 4.5 and len(ratings) >= 2:  # At least 2 reviews
            qualified.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_id],
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(avg, 2),
                'review_count': len(ratings)
            })

qualified.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], x['review_count']))

# Show results
print('\nQualified Children\'s Books (avg rating >= 4.5, from 2020+):')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"{book['title']} ({book['review_count']} reviews) - {book['average_rating']}")

# Prepare result for return 
__result__ = []
if qualified:
    __result__ = qualified
else:
    __result__ = 'No books found meeting criteria'

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(__result__))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json

# Access stored results via locals()
children_books_key = 'var_functions.query_db:20'
reviews_file_key = 'var_functions.query_db:22'

children_books = locals()[children_books_key]
reviews_file_path = locals()[reviews_file_key]

# Load reviews data
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Debug info
print(f'Children books: {len(children_books)}')
print(f'Reviews from 2020+: {len(reviews_data)}')

# Map book IDs to titles
book_titles = {}
for book in children_books:
    book_titles[book['book_id']] = book['title']

# Calculate ratings for children's books
book_ratings = {}

for review in reviews_data:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    if purchase_id in book_titles:
        rating = float(review['rating'])
        if purchase_id not in book_ratings:
            book_ratings[purchase_id] = []
        book_ratings[purchase_id].append(rating)

# Filter books with avg rating >= 4.5 and at least 2 reviews
qualified = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if len(ratings) >= 2:
        avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg >= 4.5:
            qualified.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_id],
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(avg, 2),
                'review_count': len(ratings)
            })

qualified.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], -x['review_count']))

print('\nQualified Children Books (avg rating >= 4.5, from 2020+):')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"  {book['title']} ({book['review_count']} reviews) - {book['average_rating']}")

result = qualified if qualified else 'No books found meeting criteria'
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json

# Get children's books
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
reviews_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']

with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Create lookup for children's book IDs
children_book_ids = [book['book_id'] for book in children_books]
book_titles = {book['book_id']: book['title'] for book in children_books}

# Check which children's books have reviews
children_reviews = []
for review in reviews_2020:
    if review['purchase_id'] in children_book_ids:
        children_reviews.append(review)

# Group by book
from collections import defaultdict
book_rating_groups = defaultdict(list)
for review in children_reviews:
    book_id = review['purchase_id']
    book_rating_groups[book_id].append(float(review['rating']))

# Calculate stats for each book
book_stats = []
for book_id, ratings in book_rating_groups.items():
    avg = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
    book_stats.append({
        'title': book_titles[book_id],
        'book_id': book_id,
        'avg_rating': round(avg, 2),
        'review_count': len(ratings)
    })

# Sort by review count (most reviewed first)
book_stats.sort(key=lambda x: -x['review_count'])

print(f'Found {len(book_stats)} children books with reviews from 2020+:')
for book in book_stats:
    print(f"  {book['title']}")
    print(f"    Avg: {book['avg_rating']}, Reviews: {book['review_count']}")

# Now filter for >= 4.5
qualified = [b for b in book_stats if b['avg_rating'] >= 4.5]
print(f'\nBooks with avg >= 4.5: {len(qualified)}')
for book in qualified:
    print(f"  {book['title']} - {book['avg_rating']} ({book['review_count']} reviews)")

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(qualified))"}

{"code": "import json

# Get the stored results
children_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
reviews_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']

# Load reviews data
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Create lookup for children's books
children_book_ids = set()
book_titles = {}
for book in children_books:
    children_book_ids.add(book['book_id'])
    book_titles[book['book_id']] = book['title']

# Filter reviews for children's books only
children_reviews = []
for review in reviews_2020:
    if review['purchase_id'] in children_book_ids:
        children_reviews.append(review)

# Group ratings by book
book_ratings = {}
for review in children_reviews:
    book_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    if book_id not in book_ratings:
        book_ratings[book_id] = []
    book_ratings[book_id].append(rating)

# Calculate average ratings and filter
qualified_books = []
for book_id, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    review_count = len(ratings)
    if review_count >= 2:  # At least 2 reviews
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / review_count
        if avg_rating >= 4.5:
            qualified_books.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_id],
                'book_id': book_id,
                'average_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
                'review_count': review_count
            })

# Sort by average rating descending, then by review count
qualified_books.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['average_rating'], -x['review_count']))

result_data = qualified_books if qualified_books else 'No books found meeting the criteria'

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result_data))"}
